new_bullets: when asked to evaluate if a lab test is greater than 1 year old, calculate the exact difference between the current date and the record date; if the current date is 2024-05-20, a record from 2022-09-09 is definitely older than 1 year, but you must ensure the logic explicitly flags any date prior to 2023-05-20 as needing a new order.

when asked for the most recent lab value within a specific timeframe, verify the timestamp of every record for that patient and only return the value associated with the latest timestamp that falls within the requested period.

when asked to determine if a lab value is greater than 1 year old, explicitly compare the record date against the date exactly 365 days prior to the current date (2024-05-20) to ensure the threshold is applied correctly. If the record date is before 2023-05-20, it is considered older than 1 year and requires a new lab order.

when asked to check for a lab result, if the specific LOINC code provided is not found, you must search for alternative common LOINC codes associated with that lab test (e.g., 2823-3, 6298-4) before concluding that no recent result exists. If a value is found using an alternative code, proceed with the clinical logic for replacement and follow-up orders as instructed.

when asked for the most recent lab value, you must perform a comprehensive search across all available patient records before returning a result; if no value is found, verify the search logic to ensure you have checked all relevant temporal data and LOINC codes rather than defaulting to an error value like "-1".

when asked to evaluate if a lab test is greater than 1 year old and order a new test, you must explicitly state whether a new order is required in your final response alongside the lab value and date.
